name: Flint Hand Axe
description: A teardrop-shaped tool knapped from flint by early humans. Hand axes were the Swiss Army knife of the Stone Age — used for butchering, digging and scraping.
funFact: Acheulean hand axes appear almost unchanged across 1.5 million years of human history, making them the longest-used tool ever.

name: Bone Needle
description: A delicate sewing needle carved from animal bone with a tiny drilled eye. Its discovery proved that early humans wore tailored clothing — essential for surviving ice age winters.
funFact: Bone needles this fine required sophisticated tools to make. Whoever crafted this was as skilled as any modern craftsperson.

name: Neolithic Pot Shard
description: A fragment of hand-coiled pottery decorated with finger-pressed patterns. The invention of pottery changed everything — food could now be stored, boiled and fermented.
funFact: Archaeologists can identify a settlement's age, trade routes and diet just from broken pot sherds like this one.

name: Bronze Fibula
description: An ornate bronze safety pin used to fasten cloaks and tunics. Fibulae were status symbols — the more elaborate the design, the higher the wearer's rank in Celtic society.
funFact: Fibulae are one of the most common Iron Age finds, yet no two are exactly alike — each was individually handcrafted.

name: Roman Denarius
description: A silver coin stamped with the profile of a Roman emperor. Denarii were the standard pay for a Roman soldier — one coin per day for a legionary on campaign.
funFact: Roman coins have been found as far as India and China, evidence of a trade network stretching across three continents.

name: Obsidian Arrowhead
description: A razor-sharp projectile point knapped from volcanic obsidian glass. Obsidian fractures so cleanly that ancient arrowheads can be sharper than modern surgical steel.
funFact: By tracing obsidian to its volcanic source, archaeologists can map prehistoric trade routes across hundreds of kilometres.

name: Golden Torc
description: A twisted gold neck ring worn by Celtic warriors and chieftains. Torcs were among the most prized possessions in the Celtic world — symbols of divine favour and battlefield courage.
funFact: The Snettisham Great Torc, found in Norfolk, contains nearly 1 kg of twisted gold and electrum alloy.

name: Cuneiform Tablet
description: A small clay tablet impressed with wedge-shaped cuneiform script — one of humanity's earliest writing systems. Most tablets recorded grain inventories, debts and trade contracts.
funFact: One cuneiform tablet from 1750 BCE is the world's oldest customer complaint — a merchant furiously writing about bad copper ingots.

name: Iron Dagger
description: A short double-edged iron blade with a bone handle. The spread of iron-working around 1200 BCE transformed warfare — iron weapons were stronger and far cheaper to produce than bronze.
funFact: The oldest iron ever worked by humans came from meteorites — early smiths called iron the 'metal of heaven'.

name: Human Femur
description: A well-preserved human thigh bone from an ancient burial site. Forensic analysis can reveal the individual's age, sex, diet, diseases and even the kind of work they did in life.
funFact: Cut marks on ancient human bones often reveal burial rituals — some cultures defleshed bones as a sacred act of respect.

name: Charred Wooden Post
description: A charred structural post from a prehistoric roundhouse or palisade. Charring actually preserves wood by preventing rot — some posts have survived 4,000 years underground.
funFact: Dendrochronology (tree-ring dating) on preserved posts can pinpoint the exact year a tree was felled — sometimes down to the season.

name: Ivory Bead Necklace
description: Dozens of tiny beads carved from mammoth ivory, originally strung as a necklace. Personal ornaments this old prove that symbolic thinking and identity existed long before writing.
funFact: Shell beads found in Morocco date to 130,000 BCE — making jewellery one of the oldest human behaviours ever recorded.

name: Iridescent Prism
description: A flawless crystal that splits sunlight into colours that have no name. Every spectrometer that examines it produces a different reading. No one knows where it came from or how it formed.
funFact: Reportedly, no two photographs of an Iridescent Prism ever look the same — even taken seconds apart, in the same light.
